summary: Dan Luu argues that writing advice framed as "how to write" is typically just "write how I write" in disguise, which fails because successful writing styles vary enormously across dimensions like length, tone, detail level, and structure. He demonstrates this by comparing Joel Spolsky, Paul Graham, Steve Yegge, and Julia Evans—all highly successful but with fundamentally different approaches. Rather than copying others' superficial techniques (which usually backfires), writers should start from their own goals and aptitudes, then develop processes and styles that serve those goals.
key_points: Successful programming bloggers have vastly different styles across topic selection, length, prose style, humor, technical detail, and evidence presentation
Most writing advice fails to capture what actually makes someone's writing work, similar to how dance instructors' verbal cues often don't match what they actually do
Copying superficial aspects without understanding underlying principles leads to poor results—people copy the wrong things
Writing style should emerge from your goals and what you're good at, not from imitating others
What's popular isn't necessarily what you should optimize for—different goals require different trade-offs
money_quotes: Copying what's worked for someone else is unlikely to work for you, making 'write how I write' bad advice.
Most writing advice similarly fails to capture what's important... most people copy entirely the wrong thing.
A wide variety of things can work decently well. Though, if popularity is the goal, then I've probably made a sub-optimal choice on length compared to Julia and on prose style when compared to Paul.

The more general issue is that a person who doesn't understand the thing they're trying to copy will end up copying unimportant superficial aspects of what somebody else is doing and miss the fundamentals that drive the superficial aspects.
You should start from your goals and what you're good at and go from there, not listen to somebody who's going to tell you to write like them.
key_insight: Effective writing emerges from aligning your process and style with your specific goals and aptitudes rather than copying successful writers' superficial techniques, which inevitably fails because it misses the underlying principles that make their approach work for them.
